cards.front: Compare traditional networking and controller-based networking.
cards.back: Traditional: each device runs both control plane (route/STP/etc.) and data plane locally. Configured per-device via CLI. Controller-based (SDN): centralized controller computes/programs the network; devices focus on data forwarding. Programmatic (API-driven) policy and consistency at scale.

cards.front: Define control plane, data plane, and management plane.
cards.back: Control plane: decisions about where packets go (routing protocols, STP, ARP). Data plane (forwarding): actually moves packets per control-plane decisions (FIB lookup, queueing). Management plane: device admin/config (SSH, SNMP, syslog, REST API). SDN typically centralizes the control plane.

cards.front: What's the southbound interface vs northbound interface in SDN?
cards.back: Southbound (controller → devices): how the controller programs the network — OpenFlow, NETCONF, RESTCONF, OpFlex. Northbound (apps → controller): how apps/scripts ask the controller for changes — REST APIs (JSON/HTTP). Controllers translate northbound intent to southbound device config.

cards.front: What is Cisco Catalyst Center (formerly DNA Center)?
cards.back: Cisco's enterprise SDN/network management appliance. GUI + REST API + automation engine. Designs network intent; pushes to devices via NETCONF/SSH. Provides telemetry/assurance, software image management, automation workflows. Replaces Prime Infrastructure for modern campus.

Define the desired outcome (e.g., 'all marketing PCs in VLAN 50 with internet access only'); the controller figures out HOW to implement it across all devices. Continuous verification and remediation. Cisco Catalyst Center is the IBN platform for enterprise.

cards.front: What is the imperative vs declarative model in network programming?
cards.back: Imperative: tell the device exactly what commands to run, in order ('configure VLAN 10 on this switch port'). Declarative: declare the end state ('this user should be in VLAN 10') — the controller computes the device config. Declarative is more abstract and scalable.

cards.front: Three layers of SDN architecture: Application, Control, Infrastructure?
cards.back: Application layer: scripts and apps that consume the network via the northbound API (analytics, automation tools). Control layer: the SDN controller (Cisco Catalyst Center, ACI APIC, vManage). Infrastructure layer: the physical network devices (switches, routers, APs) that the controller programs via the southbound API.

cards.front: SDN underlay vs overlay vs fabric?
cards.back: Underlay: physical network of devices and links (cables, IP routing between switches). Overlay: virtual network built on top — typically VXLAN tunnels between fabric devices. Fabric: combination of overlay + underlay treated as one logical network. SD-Access uses LISP for control plane, VXLAN for data plane, and Cisco TrustSec for policy.

cards.front: Cisco SDN solutions: SD-Access, ACI, SD-WAN — what does each automate?
cards.back: SD-Access: campus LAN automation (powered by Catalyst Center). ACI (Application-Centric Infrastructure): data-center networks (powered by APIC controller). SD-WAN: WAN automation across multiple transports (powered by vManage / Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN). All three are Cisco's Digital Network Architecture (DNA) family.

cards.front: Three node types in Cisco SD-Access fabric?
cards.back: Edge node: connects end hosts to the fabric (campus access switch with SD-Access role). Border node: connects the fabric to outside networks (WAN, internet, legacy LAN). Control node: runs LISP map-server / map-resolver, tracking endpoint-to-location mappings. A single switch can play multiple roles.
